explanation_md: `remove_prefix(2)` advances the start pointer past `>>`, making `sv` refer to `"hello<<"`. Then `remove_suffix(2)` shortens the length by 2, dropping `<<`. The resulting view is `"hello"` with `size()` 5. Neither operation touches the underlying literal.

prompt_md: Why is calling `sv.data()` and passing it directly to a C function that expects a null-terminated `const char*` potentially dangerous after `remove_suffix`?

options.a: remove_suffix deallocates the characters it removes.
options.b: The view no longer ends at a null terminator, so the C function may read beyond the intended length.
options.c: string_view::data() always returns nullptr after remove_suffix.
options.d: C functions cannot accept pointers from C++ objects.

explanation_md: `remove_suffix` only shortens `sv`'s recorded length; the underlying buffer still contains all the original characters. `sv.data()` points at the first character, but there is no null terminator at position `sv.size()` (the buffer continues beyond). A C function using null termination will read those extra bytes. Option a is false — nothing is deallocated.

options.a: ends_with is not available in C++20.
options.b: sv dangles after file += "x" potentially reallocates file's buffer.
options.c: isCppFile should take const std::string& not std::string_view.
options.d: The program prints 0 because main.cppx does not end with .cpp.

explanation_md: Appending to `file` with `+=` may trigger a reallocation of `file`'s internal buffer. After reallocation, `sv` still holds the old pointer, which now refers to freed memory. Calling `isCppFile(sv)` is undefined behavior. The fix is to create the view after all modifications are done, or to pass `file` directly to `isCppFile` each time.

`starts_with("prefix")` matches the beginning of `"prefix_data"` → `true` (printed as `1`). `ends_with("data")` matches the tail → `true` (1). `starts_with("data")` does not match the beginning → `false` (0). These return `bool`, which `cout` prints as `1` or `0` by default.

options.a: Both A and B are safe.
options.b: Only A is safe, because string_view keeps the data alive.
options.c: Only B is safe, because the source string is still alive when sv is used.
options.d: Neither A nor B is safe.

explanation_md: In option A, `tmp` is destroyed when the block ends, leaving `sv` dangling. Using `sv` afterward is undefined behavior. In option B, `s` is still alive when `print` executes, so `sv` is a valid view throughout the function call. `std::string_view` never extends the lifetime of the source — the source must outlive the view.
